title: SearchOS-V1: Towards Robust Open-Domain Information-Seeking Agent Collaboration

central_claim.text: SearchOS makes search tasks, evidence, coverage, and failures explicit shared state, then uses middleware and continuous scheduling to coordinate agents around unresolved information gaps.

tldr: Long search conversations can hide what has been verified, what remains missing, and which approaches have already failed.
SearchOS reformulates an information request as relational schema completion and stores tasks, evidence, coverage, and failures outside agent conversations.
Middleware accepts evidence only when it binds to a schema cell and an anchored source span; sensors use progress and budget signals to continue, redirect, or stop work.
The system leads reported F1 metrics on WideSearch and GISA, but results use one model configuration and best-of-three reporting, while citation truth is not evaluated separately.

blocks.question: Why do capable search agents still repeat work and miss evidence?
paragraphs: A long-horizon research task requires more than issuing good queries. The system must remember which entities and attributes are complete, preserve the source for each value, expose contradictions, and avoid retrying search paths that another worker has already exhausted.

Conventional agents often keep this state in growing conversation histories. As evidence becomes buried, workers can duplicate effort, disagree about fields, overlook gaps, or leave parallel slots idle while a slow branch finishes.

blocks.question: What becomes explicit when search is treated as schema completion?
paragraphs: SearchOS converts a natural-language request into one or more related tables. Rows represent entities, columns represent requested attributes, and a citation matrix connects every populated value to a source URL and supporting excerpt. Missing cells become concrete search targets rather than vague reminders in a plan.

The system then separates global coordination from local search. An orchestrator owns schema and task mutation, explore and search agents browse, and a writer reads accumulated state. Their coordination passes through shared records rather than free-form agent-to-agent conversation.

blocks.question: How does a browser observation become shared, grounded progress?
paragraphs: Search-Oriented Context Management contains four linked stores. Frontier Task tracks dependency-aware work. The Evidence Graph stores atomic findings with values, sources, anchored spans, schema bindings, and provenance. The Coverage Map materializes the status of each known cell. Failure Memory records unsuccessful queries, inaccessible sources, rejected claims, and corrective guidance.

Before a model call, context middleware projects only the role-relevant portion of that state and adds selected skills. After browsing, evidence middleware accepts a candidate only if it binds to a schema cell and can be anchored to the observed source span. It then updates the Evidence Graph and Coverage Map atomically.

Sensor middleware measures changes in grounded coverage and evidence count, along with iteration, search, and time budgets. It can continue execution, request backfill, inject a correction, enter drain-only mode, or stop a branch. Continuous dispatch assigns newly available slots to ready gaps instead of waiting for a synchronized batch.

blocks.question: What happens when one requested table cell is still missing?
paragraphs: Suppose a comparison request has a known company row but no verified value for one attribute. The Coverage Map marks that cell missing. Frontier Task creates or selects a scoped job for the cell, and continuous dispatch sends it to an available search agent with relevant failure records and source-access skills.

A page visit alone does not fill the cell. Evidence middleware must extract a candidate value, bind it to the correct row and attribute, and anchor it to a supporting span. Once accepted, the evidence and coverage records update together. If repeated calls produce no new evidence or coverage, the sensor can redirect or stop the branch rather than letting the model repeat the same search.

blocks.question: How well does the complete system perform?
paragraphs: On WideSearch, SearchOS reports 80.3 item F1 and 56.5 row F1, compared with 76.0 and 54.5 for the strongest baseline on each metric. On GISA, the largest gain is set F1: 76.5 versus 63.1 for the strongest baseline. The system also leads the reported table and list F1 metrics.

A paired scheduling study on 10 WideSearch cases reports that continuous dispatch reduces average runtime from 629.13 to 476.34 seconds, raises slot utilization from 34.6% to 41.7%, and raises item F1 from 79.66 to 86.75. This is a subset experiment, not the main benchmark result.

A joint removal of all hierarchical skill layers lowers item F1 from 80.3 to 78.3 and row F1 from 56.5 to 53.1. The same study reports lower session time and fewer search and page calls with skills enabled, but it does not isolate which skill layer supplies the benefit.

blocks.question: What does the evaluation leave unresolved?
paragraphs: The main evaluation uses GLM-5 for agent roles, Qwen3.5-35B-A3B for evidence extraction, and reports the best of three runs for each case. It does not report mean performance or variance across those runs, and the paper does not document matched end-to-end budgets for every baseline in enough detail to settle cost fairness.

A URL and anchored excerpt preserve provenance but do not independently prove that the extracted value is true. Citation correctness is not reported as a separate metric. The middleware analysis shows three representative interventions rather than a controlled middleware-removal experiment, and the benchmarks' structured answer formats may particularly suit relational schema completion.
The authors scope V1 to externalized search state and leave large-scale skill synthesis, broader domains, multimodal search, and improved adaptation for future work.

blocks.question: Which conclusions follow from the ablations, and which do not?
paragraphs: The paper provides bounded engineering evidence for making research state explicit. The schema, evidence gate, progress sensors, and scheduler define inspectable responsibilities, while the scheduling and skill ablations support specific efficiency and quality benefits on their tested subsets.

The main benchmark comparison evaluates the complete system, so it cannot assign the overall gain to middleware, coverage-aware scheduling, schema planning, or skills individually. The authors connect the recall gains to coverage-aware dispatch; that explanation is consistent with the result pattern but remains an interpretation rather than a controlled causal finding.

claims.text: SearchOS externalizes tasks, evidence, coverage, and failures as shared system state used to coordinate long-horizon information seeking.
SearchOS represents a request as relational schema completion and associates each populated value with a source URL and anchored excerpt.
SOCM consists of Frontier Task, an Evidence Graph, a Coverage Map, and Failure Memory with role-specific projections.
Evidence middleware accepts a browsing candidate only when it binds to a schema cell and anchors to a source span, then updates evidence and coverage atomically.
Pipeline-parallel orchestration immediately assigns released execution slots to ready unresolved gaps instead of waiting for synchronized batches.
SearchOS-V1 contains 280 orchestrator, strategy, and access skills selected according to task and source.
SearchOS reports 80.3 item F1 on WideSearch and 76.5 set F1 on GISA, leading the evaluated baselines on those metrics.
On the scheduling subset, continuous dispatch reduces average time from 629.13 to 476.34 seconds and raises item F1 from 79.66 to 86.75 relative to batch scheduling.
Enabling the complete hierarchical skill system raises item F1 by 2.0 points and row F1 by 3.4 points in the reported Max@3 ablation.
The authors attribute the main WideSearch recall gain to coverage-aware dispatch reducing missing cells.
The experiments do not separately establish that every schema-bound anchored excerpt supports a factually correct extracted value.
The paper does not document matched end-to-end search, model-call, time, and compute budgets for every baseline in enough detail to establish cost-fair main benchmark comparisons.
The three representative middleware-intervention trajectories are not a controlled middleware-removal experiment and do not isolate middleware's causal contribution to benchmark performance.
Because WideSearch and GISA score structured table, set, list, and item outputs, their formats may particularly suit SearchOS's relational schema-completion design.
The inspectable shared records, schema-bound evidence, progress sensors, and subset ablations provide bounded engineering evidence for externalizing research state in the tested SearchOS setup.
The evaluation does not establish the same reliability gains across other model backbones, multimodal sources, or unstructured task families.

visuals.title: From an open request to inspectable missing cells
visuals.question: What becomes explicit when a natural-language search request is represented as relational schema completion?

items.label: Natural-language request
items.detail: The user asks for entities, attributes, comparisons, and supporting sources without yet defining a durable search state.

items.label: Related tables
items.detail: Rows represent entities, columns represent requested attributes, and relations connect the tables needed for the answer.

items.label: Materialized coverage
items.detail: Each known cell has an explicit status such as missing, filled, uncertain, unreachable, or conflicting.

items.label: Citation binding
items.detail: A populated value is associated with its source URL and an anchored supporting excerpt rather than only appearing in conversation history.

visuals.interpretation: The representation change turns vague remaining work into addressable cells and keeps provenance next to each accepted value. It gives the scheduler a shared target outside any one agent's conversation.
limitations: A source URL and anchored excerpt preserve provenance but do not independently prove that the extracted value is true.
Coverage of materialized rows cannot prove exhaustive discovery in an open-set task.
Structured benchmark formats may particularly suit this relational representation.
visuals.alt_text: Parallel view of a natural-language request becoming related entity-attribute tables, explicit cell coverage states, and value-to-citation bindings.
visuals.fallback: SearchOS converts a natural-language request into related tables whose rows are entities and columns are requested attributes. Every known cell receives a coverage status, and each accepted value is associated with a source URL and anchored excerpt. This makes missing work and provenance explicit, although it does not prove exhaustive discovery or factual truth.

visuals.title: The SearchOS evidence-and-control loop
visuals.question: How does SearchOS turn browsing activity into shared, grounded progress rather than more conversation history?

items.label: Frontier Task schedules a gap
items.detail: Dependency-aware task state selects a ready unresolved schema cell and continuously assigns available execution slots.

items.label: Context middleware projects state
items.detail: The worker receives its assignment, relevant evidence and coverage, prior failures, and selected skills instead of the full global history.

items.label: Worker searches
items.detail: An explore or search agent browses under that scoped context and returns candidate findings or a failed attempt.

items.label: Evidence middleware gates
items.detail: A candidate is accepted only when it binds to the intended schema cell and anchors to an observed source span; rejection can return corrective guidance.

items.label: Shared stores update
items.detail: Accepted evidence updates the Evidence Graph and Coverage Map atomically, while task outcomes and failed attempts update Frontier Task and Failure Memory.

items.label: Sensors choose the next transition
items.detail: Coverage growth, evidence growth, and budgets can trigger continuation, backfill, correction, drain-only mode, branch termination, or synthesis.

visuals.interpretation: The stores and middleware form a feedback loop rather than a one-way pipeline. Browsing does not automatically become evidence: accepted or rejected observations change shared state, and sensors use that new state to schedule, redirect, stop, or synthesize.
limitations: An anchored excerpt and URL preserve provenance but do not independently establish that the extracted value is true.
Relational schemas may not represent every open-ended information-seeking task equally well.
Coverage measures known materialized rows and cannot prove exhaustive discovery in open-set tasks.
The paper does not independently ablate middleware's contribution to the main benchmark scores.
The diagram omits the internal structure and maintenance cost of the 280-skill library.
This is an original Paper Atlas composition based on the cited paper, not a reproduction of a source figure; the CC BY 4.0 paper is attributed through the source references.
visuals.alt_text: Feedback loop connecting Frontier Task scheduling, role-scoped context, worker search, an evidence acceptance gate, atomic Evidence Graph and Coverage Map updates, Failure Memory, and progress sensors that return work to scheduling or stop it.
visuals.fallback: Frontier Task schedules a ready unresolved cell. Context middleware gives the worker relevant shared state, failures, and skills. After search, evidence middleware accepts a candidate only when it binds to the cell and an anchored source span. Accepted evidence updates the Evidence Graph and Coverage Map together, while failures update task and failure records. Sensors inspect progress and budgets, then continue, correct, backfill, stop, or allow synthesis.

visuals.title: SearchOS leads the reported comparison metrics
visuals.question: How large are the reported SearchOS gains over the strongest baseline on the highlighted WideSearch and GISA metrics?

items.label: WideSearch item F1 · strongest baseline
items.detail: Strongest reported baseline value for item F1.

items.label: WideSearch row F1 · strongest baseline
items.detail: Strongest reported baseline value for row F1.

items.label: GISA set F1 · strongest baseline
items.detail: Strongest reported baseline value for set F1.

visuals.interpretation: SearchOS leads the strongest reported baseline on each highlighted metric, with the largest displayed gap on GISA set F1. The comparison evaluates the complete system and does not identify which component causes the gain.
limitations: The paper reports the best of three runs for each case rather than means and variance.
Matched end-to-end budgets are not documented in enough detail to settle cost fairness for every baseline.
The evaluation uses one model configuration and does not establish the same gains across other backbones or task families.
visuals.alt_text: Three paired dot comparisons: WideSearch item F1 is 76.0 for the strongest baseline and 80.3 for SearchOS; WideSearch row F1 is 54.5 and 56.5; GISA set F1 is 63.1 and 76.5.
visuals.fallback: On WideSearch, the strongest baseline and SearchOS score 76.0 and 80.3 item F1, and 54.5 and 56.5 row F1. On GISA, they score 63.1 and 76.5 set F1. These are complete-system, best-of-three comparisons under one model configuration.

visuals.title: Batch waiting versus continuous dispatch
visuals.question: What changes when released execution slots receive ready work immediately instead of waiting for a synchronized batch?

items.label: Batch scheduling
items.detail: A new batch waits for the current synchronized group to finish. On the 10-case subset, average runtime is 629.13 seconds, slot utilization is 34.6%, and item F1 is 79.66.

items.label: Continuous dispatch
items.detail: Each released slot immediately receives a ready unresolved gap. On the same subset, average runtime is 476.34 seconds, slot utilization is 41.7%, and item F1 is 86.75.

visuals.interpretation: Continuous dispatch reduces idle waiting in the paired subset and is associated there with lower runtime, higher slot utilization, and higher item F1. The experiment supports this scheduling comparison, not every component of SearchOS.
limitations: The scheduling study covers 10 WideSearch cases and is not the main benchmark evaluation.
The comparison does not isolate middleware, schema planning, or skills.
Subset item F1 values should not replace the main WideSearch benchmark values.
visuals.alt_text: Side-by-side comparison: batch scheduling waits for synchronized completion and reports 629.13 seconds, 34.6 percent utilization, and 79.66 item F1; continuous dispatch refills released slots and reports 476.34 seconds, 41.7 percent utilization, and 86.75 item F1 on the same 10-case subset.
visuals.fallback: In the 10-case scheduling subset, batch scheduling waits for a synchronized group to finish and reports 629.13 seconds average runtime, 34.6% slot utilization, and 79.66 item F1. Continuous dispatch immediately refills released slots and reports 476.34 seconds, 41.7% utilization, and 86.75 item F1. This subset comparison does not isolate the other SearchOS components.

glossary.definition: Representing a search request as linked tables whose entity rows, attribute values, and citation cells must be completed.
A dependency-aware pool of pending, running, and terminal search jobs tied to unresolved schema cells.
A store of atomic values, sources, anchored excerpts, schema bindings, confidence, provenance, and support or conflict relations.
The materialized fill state of known schema cells, including missing, filled, uncertain, unreachable, and conflicting values.
The system layer that prepares role-specific context, validates evidence-producing observations, and monitors progress and budgets around model and tool calls.
Assigning a ready task to each released execution slot immediately instead of waiting for every worker in a synchronized batch.

revision_notes: Initial explainer draft based on arXiv:2607.15257v1.
Centered the explanation on explicit shared state, evidence gating, and the scheduler control loop.
Kept main benchmark results separate from scheduling and skill subset ablations.
Marked citation truth, middleware causality, baseline budget fairness, structured-benchmark fit, and cross-model generality with explicit epistemic labels.
Verified the source paper's CC BY 4.0 license; the explainer visual is an attributed original composition.
